content.text: Access scoping

narrows what a connector can touch within a goal. Where OAuth authorization grants access to a service ("this goal can use Gmail"), scoping restricts access within that service ("this goal can only read emails from @acmecorp.com domain and only write to the Outbox label").

Scoping is the primary tool for reducing blast radius. A goal that can only touch a specific Drive folder cannot accidentally read or overwrite other folders. A Chrome connector scoped to a single domain cannot follow links to unrelated sites. Scoping makes goals safer to run, safer to auto-approve, and easier to audit.

Scoping is set in two places: at the connector configuration level (applies to all goals using that connector in a workspace or plugin) and at the goal text level (applies to that specific goal invocation). Goal-level scoping is more granular and takes precedence.
Why Access Scoping Matters

Connector authorization is coarse-grained — Gmail access means access to your entire inbox, Drive access means access to all files you can see. Without scoping, a goal's reach is as broad as the connector's OAuth grant. For most goals, that broad reach is unnecessary and exposes data that the goal has no business touching.
Principle of least privilege

Each goal should only be able to access the data it needs for its stated purpose. A deal memo skill needs: the email threads for one account, one Drive folder for output. It does not need access to all Gmail, all of Drive, all Slack channels. Scoping enforces least-privilege at the goal level.
Narrower access = smaller attack surface if a goal's output is accidentally shared
Narrower access = faster execution — the engine does not scan irrelevant data
Narrower access = easier to audit — the action log shows activity only in the declared scope
Narrower access = auto-approve friendly — IT and compliance are more comfortable auto-approving goals with explicit, narrow scopes
Scoping vs sandbox

Sandbox and scoping are complementary, not alternatives. Sandbox prevents writes from executing during testing. Scoping limits what the engine can read or write at all, in both sandbox and live mode. Use sandbox to test; use scoping to limit live access to only what the goal needs.
Scoping Google Drive Access
Drive scoping can restrict by folder path, by file type, and by operation (read-only vs read/write). Scoping is set in the goal text, the connector configuration, or the plugin manifest.
Folder path scoping

In goal text:
Only access files within the "Deal Memos / Acme Corp" folder in Drive.
Do not read or write to any other folder.

Or more precise:
Read files from "Accounts / Acme Corp / Documents".
Write the output to "Deal Memos / Q4 2025" — do not create files elsewhere.

The engine respects folder scope instructions in goal text. If a connector search would return files outside the stated scope, the engine does not read them. If a write operation would create a file outside the stated scope, the engine uses the specified destination instead.
Read-only scoping

In goal text:
Read the "Contracts" folder in Drive to extract clause summaries.
Do not create, modify, or delete any files in Drive.
Write the summary to an inline Cowork output only — not to Drive.

Declaring the goal as Drive-read-only prevents accidental writes even when the Drive connector has write permission via OAuth. The engine treats the read-only instruction as a hard constraint — a write action that would otherwise be triggered by the goal's reasoning is blocked.
File type scoping
Restrict to Google Docs only: "Only read Google Doc files — ignore Sheets, Slides, and PDFs"
Restrict to Sheets: "Read only the Sheets in the specified folder — ignore other file types"
Restrict by name pattern: "Only read files whose names start with 'Q3-'"
These restrictions are enforced by the engine's goal reasoning, not by the API — the engine skips non-matching files
Connector-level folder restriction

Workspace admins can set a folder restriction in the Drive connector configuration (Settings → Connectors → Drive → Folder Restriction). When set, all goals using the Drive connector in this workspace can only access files within the specified folder tree — even if a goal text requests broader access.
Connector-level restriction overrides goal-level scope — it is a ceiling, not a default
Useful for shared project workspaces where Cowork should only touch project-related files
When connector restriction and goal scope both apply, the more restrictive scope wins
Scoping Slack Access
Slack scoping limits which channels the engine can read from and post to. Without scoping, a Slack connector with workspace access can read any channel the user is a member of.
Channel allow-list

In goal text:
Only read from #product-eng and #design channels.
Post summaries to #weekly-digest only.
Do not access any other channels.
The engine respects channel restrictions in goal text. A search-workspace instruction that would normally scan all channels is limited to the declared allow-list.
Read-only and post-only scoping
Mode
What it allows
Example use case
Read-only
Read channel history and search; cannot post
Digest goals that summarise channels without posting
Post-only
Post messages; cannot read history
Alert goals that post notifications without consuming message history
Read + post
Full channel access within the allow-list
Standard digest + post goals
DM scoping
By default, Slack connector access includes direct messages. For goals where DM access is not needed, restrict it explicitly:

In goal text:
Only access public channels — do not read or send direct messages.

Or in connector settings:
Disable DM access for this connector instance.
Scoping Chrome Connector Access
Chrome scoping limits which URLs the engine can navigate to and whether it can follow links. Without scoping, a Chrome connector goal can follow links across the open web indefinitely.
Domain allow-list

In goal text:
Only load pages within docs.company.com and status.company.com.
Do not navigate to any other domain, even if links on these pages lead elsewhere.
URL prefix scoping

In goal text:
Only read pages at URLs beginning with https://internal-tools.company.com/reports/.
Do not access any other URL paths on that domain or any other domain.
Link-follow depth and no-follow
No-follow: "Read the target URL only — do not follow any links on the page" — keeps Chrome access to a single page
Depth-limited: "Follow links up to 2 levels deep from the starting URL" — bounds the browsing tree
Same-domain-only follow: "Follow links, but only if they stay within docs.company.com" — prevents following external links
Scoping Gmail Access
Gmail scoping narrows which threads the engine reads and which actions it can perform. Unlike Drive and Slack, Gmail scoping is primarily expressed through search query constraints in the goal text.
Sender and recipient domain scoping

In goal text:
Only read email threads where at least one participant has an @acmecorp.com address.
Do not read any thread that does not involve Acme Corp contacts.
Date range and label scoping

Scope by date:
Only read emails received in the last 30 days. Ignore older threads.

Scope by label:
Only read threads in the "Client Correspondence" label. Ignore inbox and other labels.

Scope by type:
Ignore automated emails, marketing, and calendar notifications.
Only read threads that contain substantive human-authored messages.
Write operation scoping
Draft-only: "Draft all replies — do not send any email" — prevents send actions even when email connector is authorized
Label-only: "Only apply labels — do not archive, delete, or modify message content"
Specific recipient domain: "Only send email to @company.com recipients — reject any goal instruction to send to external addresses"
Goal-Level vs Connector-Level Scoping
Scoping can be set at two levels, each with different reach and precedence.
Level
Where set
Who controls
Overridable?
Best for
Connector configuration
Settings → Connectors → [connector] → Scope
Workspace admin
No — ceiling for all goals
Org-wide restrictions (e.g. Drive access only within company folder)
Plugin manifest
Plugin connector declaration
Plugin author
No — ceiling for plugin goals
Plugin-specific restrictions (e.g. plugin only touches Sales folder)
Goal text
Written in the goal
Goal author
Yes — user can widen within connector ceiling
Per-goal scoping for specific tasks
The connector-level scope is the ceiling — no goal can access more than the connector configuration allows. Goal-level scope can be narrower than the connector ceiling but not wider.

If a connector is configured to allow full Drive access but a goal needs only one folder, add folder scoping in the goal text. The goal will run with the narrower scope even though the connector could allow more. Future goals that need the full drive can still use it — the goal-level scope does not change the connector configuration.
Scoping in Multi-Connector Goals

When a goal uses multiple connectors, each connector is scoped independently. You can have a narrow Drive scope alongside a broad Gmail scope, or a read-only Slack scope alongside a fully writable Drive scope. The engine applies each connector's scope separately for each connector call it makes.
Example: multi-connector goal with mixed scoping

Task goal with explicit scoping per connector:

Gmail: Search for threads involving @acmecorp.com in the last 60 days.
       [Scope: read-only, @acmecorp.com domain filter, last 60 days only]

Drive: Read the "Accounts / Acme Corp" folder for existing notes.
       [Scope: read-only, single named folder only]

Drive (output): Write the deal memo to the "Deal Memos / Q4 2025" folder.
       [Scope: write to this folder only, no reads allowed on output folder]

Slack: Post the memo summary to #sales-team.
       [Scope: post to #sales-team only, no channel history reading]

Do not read or write to any connector resource outside the scopes listed above.

Each connector operation is bounded by its stated scope. The engine does not share scope across connectors — a narrow Slack scope does not affect the Gmail scope, and a read-only Gmail scope does not prevent Drive writes.
Declaring output-only connector access

A common pattern is to read from one connector and write to another, with neither connector having overlapping access. For example, a research goal that reads via Chrome and writes to Drive should scope Chrome to read-only and Drive to write-only for the output folder:

Chrome: Read the following URLs: [list of URLs].
        Do not follow links. Read-only — no form submissions or clicks.

Drive: Create a new Google Doc in the "Research Outputs" folder with the synthesised findings.
       Do not read any existing files in Drive for this goal — Chrome is the only data source.
The explicit "do not read any existing files in Drive" instruction prevents the engine from searching Drive for context — it limits Drive to output-only for this goal.
Scoping for Compliance and Regulated Data

In organizations subject to data regulations (GDPR, HIPAA, SOC 2, financial compliance), scoping is not optional — it is a control requirement. The goal author is responsible for ensuring that goals do not access regulated data outside the defined scope. Workspace admins enforce minimum scoping standards.
PII and sensitive data scoping
Goals that might encounter personally identifiable information (PII) should scope their access to the minimum dataset and explicitly instruct the engine how to handle PII in outputs:

Task goal with PII scoping:

Read the "HR Onboarding" folder in Drive (HR team access only — this connector
is authorized under the HR workspace, not the general workspace).
[Scope: single named folder, HR workspace connector only]

Extract the list of new hire names and start dates for Q4 2025.
Do not extract any other personal information (salary, address, SSN, DOB, etc.).
Output: write the name + start date list to the "New Hire Announcements" folder.
Do not include personal information beyond name and start date in the output.
The scoping here is two-layered: the connector is authorized for a specific workspace context (HR), and the goal text further restricts what data is extracted and what is excluded from the output.
Audit logging of scoped access

When a goal declares explicit scoping, the action log records the declared scope alongside each connector call. Auditors can verify that the engine only accessed the declared resources and that any out-of-scope access attempt was blocked by the engine's reasoning constraints.
Action log entry for a scoped Drive read: "Drive read: folder 'Accounts / Acme Corp' — scoped, 3 files read"
Action log entry for a blocked out-of-scope attempt: "Drive read blocked: file 'Payroll 2025' is outside declared scope"
Gmail scoped read: "Gmail search: @acmecorp.com last 60 days — scoped, 14 threads read, 2 threads outside scope skipped"
Verifying Scope Effectiveness

Sandbox mode is the primary tool for verifying that a goal's scope is working as intended. Before running a scoped goal live, run it in sandbox and review the action log to confirm that the engine only accessed the declared resources.
Scope verification checklist
Run the goal in sandbox mode
Open the action log in the sandbox report
Verify that every Drive read is within the declared folder scope
Verify that every Slack read/post is within the declared channel list
Verify that every Chrome navigation is within the declared domain scope
Verify that every Gmail access is within the declared search scope
If any reads appear outside scope: tighten the goal text and re-run sandbox
If the engine correctly skipped out-of-scope resources: the scope is working correctly
What to look for in the action log

A well-scoped goal produces an action log with a small, specific set of connector calls. If the action log shows a large number of reads across many folders, channels, or domains, the scope may be too broad.
Too many Gmail reads: the search query is too broad — add sender domain filter, date range, or label restriction
Too many Drive reads: the folder scope is too wide — specify a more precise folder path or file name pattern
Too many Chrome navigations: link-following is too permissive — add no-follow instruction or domain restriction
No reads at all: the scope may be too narrow — the engine cannot find any data matching the declared scope; check folder paths and search terms
Summary

Access scoping limits what a connector can touch within a goal — by folder, by channel, by domain, by operation type, or by data characteristics. Scoping narrows blast radius, speeds up execution, and makes goals safer to auto-approve.
Drive: scope by folder path, file type, and read vs read/write
Slack: scope by channel allow-list, read-only, or post-only
Chrome: scope by domain allow-list, URL prefix, and link-follow depth
Gmail: scope by sender/recipient domain, date range, label, and write operation type
Connector-level scope (set by admin) is the ceiling; goal-level scope can be narrower but not wider
Use scoping alongside sandbox and approval prompts for defense-in-depth — all three mechanisms are complementary
